speaker: interviewer
text: Today is October 10, 1996. This interview is with Mrs. Pearl Barach nee Spiegelstein. The interviewer is Ann Leib-- L-I-E-B-- in Brooklyn, New York, The United States of America. The language is English. Hello, my name is Ann Lieb-- L-I-E-B-- and I'm interviewing Mrs. Pearl Barach on October 10th, 1996. Could you please tell me your name and spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Pearl Barach-- B-A-R-A-C-- like in 'candy'-- H.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you please tell me your maiden name and spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name-- my maiden name is Spiegelstein-- S-P-I-E-G-E-L-S-T-E-I-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you please tell me the date of your birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: December the 15th, 1923.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you please tell me the town and country of your birth and spell the name of the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in-- in Wohyn, Poland-- Wohyn-- W-O-H-Y-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you please tell me your father's name and a little bit about your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Jacob-- Jacob [? Isaac, ?] and he was a wealthy man. He was a good business man, and he did everything to make a nice living for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me a little bit about his business interests?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, he had rented a mill-- one to make flour with three more partners. For-- and he-- and he sold it to wholesale places-- to bakeries and stores. He made flour.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he home a lot with the children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not during the day, but he came home early, like 3 o'clock, 4 o'clock sometimes. And he spent a lot of time with the children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you please tell me your mother's name and her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name was Braina Ruchel Nissenbaum-- N-I-S-E-N-B-A-U-M.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me a little bit about your mother? What was she like?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was a good person. She was beautiful-- blonde, blue eyes. And she was a good housekeeper. She took good care of the children. And she had a lot of friends, and she was religious.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me the names of your siblings and a little bit about them?

speaker: interviewee
text: My oldest brother was Abraham Chaim, and next to him was Eliezer. And then I was the third, and I had a brother Joshua. And the next was Moshe, and I had a little baby sister-- she was born in 1938-- Chana.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your brothers go away to yeshiva?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, but they went to a cheder.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me about your education.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to public school, and I finished just before the war broke out. And I went to Bais Yaakov School for Girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you study at the public school?

speaker: interviewee
text: The public school, we studied English, math, writing, reading. We had gym. I-- sew-- we learned how to sew and geography, history-- everything, but you have to learn.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have classes with Jewish children and Gentile children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we did.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the Gentile children treat the Jewish children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very good. They're very-- we were very good friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the names of any of your Gentile classmates?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I remember one-- [? Zosha, ?] and I can't remember too many.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the teachers-- were they Gentiles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly were Gentiles. And one Jewish teacher was [? Regina. ?] I don't remember her name now. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the Gentile teachers treat the Jewish students?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very good-- the same-- no different than the other-- the Gentiles.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you play with any Gentile children outside of school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I had a few friends from school, from the class.

speaker: interviewer
text: In your town, do you ever remember Gentile children taunting or hitting the Jewish children?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't remember that, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the relationship between your father and the Gentiles in-- in the area? Did he do business with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he did. He had a lot of friends, many friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many Jewish families lived in your hometown?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was about around 300 to 350-- between 300 and 400, I would say.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many synagogues were there in the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was one big synagogue, and there was a little-- two shtiebels.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did your family pray?

speaker: interviewee
text: My family prayed on the holidays and on-- on Shabbos. They prayed at my uncle's shtiebel. He gave up a room-- a big room-- for the holidays and for Shabbos. During the week, they went to the big shul.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me about your Bais Yaakov education.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, in Bais Yaakov, we learned how to read Hebrew, write Jewish, and we used to go-- every Saturday, we had minyan for the girls only, and that's about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was Friday night dinner like in your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Friday, my mother used to get up very early. She used to be challahs and cake, and she cooked-- made gefilte fish. She cooked, and she did everything. And we sat down at the dinners Friday night. My mother lit the candles, and we had the-- my father made kiddish. And we were singing and eating, and we enjoyed it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Shabbos morning, you went to the big shul.

speaker: interviewee
text: Shabbos morning, I went to do the Bais Yaakov's school. We had [BACKROUND NOISE] a minyan, we davened. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do in the afternoon?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the afternoon, we played with the children, with the friends. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the holiday of Passover-- Pesach. What do you remember as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, Passover was a very big holiday. We had to change the dishes and do a lot of shopping. You used to bake matzos. [INAUDIBLE]. They didn't buy a ready-made, but they used to go to a special bakery. Everything was kosher. And we had a lot to-- to prepare.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you help your mother in the household?

speaker: interviewee
text: A little bit. She'd send me places to-- to shop, you know, and to-- I helped a little, as much I could.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the Seder like in your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the Seder-- the whole family sat down, and they [INAUDIBLE]. And we drank the wine and gefilte fish. And everything we ate, and we enjoyed it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- did any of the relatives come for that holiday?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, in Europe, everybody ate by himself. They all had big families, and they ate-- in Europe wasn't like here.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about the holiday of Purim?

speaker: interviewee
text: Purim? Oh, it was a very-- [SIGHS] we were fasting. I mean, not me, but they were fasting. And then they made a big seudah, and they baked and cooked, and it was a big holiday.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever wear a costume?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't remember that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Rosh Hashanah was a holiday, and we went to shul. I did too. And Yom Kippur, we fast. [BACKROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell-- could you please tell me your address-- where you lived?

speaker: interviewee
text: I lived on Rynek number 6-- it means Marketplace number 6. And across the street was a church.

speaker: interviewer
text: You told me that this was an estate-- part of your grandfather's estate.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we live in a-- yes, my grandfather left an estate. It was, like, four apartments. My grandmother lived in one with her daughter, [? Esther ?] Spiegelstein. And then my-- we lived in the front and one side. And then the other front apartment-- another-- another sister-- my father's-- with her husband and children.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sarah Luba. And in the back, my father's niece lived there with a family, with her family. And we had a big yard. And my uncle, Sarah Luba's husband, had [? a factory ?] [? from ?] soda and seltzer in the backyard of the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many rooms were there in your apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: In my apartment, there were about three rooms. But later on, when my brothers got bigger, they fixed-- on the-- on the top-- on the attic, they fixed two more bedrooms.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there indoor plumbing in this house?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you a member of any Jewish organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your brothers belong to any Jewish organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the town have a rabbi?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I forgot. They were two rabbis, and I don't remember the names.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember anything about them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they were very religious with big beards, and they wore white socks and silk coats and a shtreimel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the Gentiles in your area-- were they always polite and friendly?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, always because they did business with the Jews. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you say that the Jews of your community were well off?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not everybody, but some were well off, and some weren't.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did the troubles first begin?

speaker: interviewee
text: The trouble first began right after they took over Poland. They gave-- the Germans came in. They gave orders to bring in all-- all the radios, the valuables, and the jewelry, and the fur coats-- everything. And then after, they burned the whole town. They threw firebombs from planes, and they burned the town.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about that day? Were you in the center of town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was in the center of town. And when we heard the bombs, we were running out. And right near my foot, a bomb fell, but I wasn't hurt. And we ran out of town to the woods, to the farms.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were any of the Jews killed in that particular bombing?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nothing that I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did this happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: This happened right after they took over Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, in '39-- 1939 in September.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your family had to give in valuables at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember your parents giving in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Whatever-- my mother had jewelry and watches, bicycle, and what else-- a radio. We had our radio with earphones, and everything in--

speaker: interviewer
text: So after the bombing, what did your family do?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to a farm-- my father's friend [? Benedict ?] Rycerski-- R-Y-C-E-R-S-K-I. And we stayed there for a few weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we came to town. My-- the-- my father-- at-- he rented the mill from the people. They gave-- they build a new house, so they gave us their old house, and we lived there. It wasn't-- you know, not really in the-- in the center of the town, but it was a little further away.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were the other Jewish families at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they all ran wherever they could-- wherever they could. I don't know, but they went to the farms, to the-- there were no houses-- not too many were-- were left-- not too many Jewish houses.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you lived on-- in this house for how long?

speaker: interviewee
text: In this house-- about a year. My grandmother died there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this-- what was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Matel Spiegelstein.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were there any new rules by the Germans? What was life like under the German occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: We have to wear our armband with a Jewish star-- a white armband with a blue Jewish star, and we could not walk all over. We had to walk in a certain-- I mean, to-- in a certain place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where the Jewish people permitted to own businesses?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not no more-- no businesses.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father work at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how-- how did the Jews feed their families?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. They-- whatever they had, they could buy bread, and have it-- I think they-- whatever they had-- money left or whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: At this time, how were the Gentiles treating your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: They all promised us, if [INAUDIBLE] then, they'll hide us. The all promised because we heard what's going on, and we knew. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We got some food from the Gentiles too. They were in the farms. They had flour, you know, they-- whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father travel to the different farms to get food, or how did you-- what-- what was the family doing at this period?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we didn't do nothing. We just got together with the people, you know, during the day. And that's all. At night, we weren't allowed to go out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you allowed to meet to daven?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they met to daven. All-- all-- only in the neighborhood. There never was a room-- any room. The shul was burned-- the shtiebel-- everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened next? What do you remember next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Next? My father sent me away to a-- a village. He knew the people, and I used to help in the kitchen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what village was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kopina-- K-O-P-I-N-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me about working.

speaker: interviewee
text: I helped out in the kitchen. I peeled potatoes. The men was working, I used-- were working-- and I used to bring out water for them to drink and food sometimes-- whatever they needed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So who was working on the farm? Was it mostly Gentiles, or?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jews separate, and Gentiles-- they were from before, but they were-- worked separate. And Ukrainians were watching us, and there one from Estonia also watching us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did this go on? How long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say about a year.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened to the Jews in that area?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were no Jews in this farm-- only the people who came from our town to work. What happened-- one day-- I-- I didn't sleep in the kitchen where everybody slept. I slept in an attic with my friend [INAUDIBLE]. And they start to gather-- gather the people to send out the people to the Ghetto Miedzyrzec. And-- and they-- and a-- and a Gentile told them that we are upstairs in the attic sleeping. And they got us too. They took us out. But and one man watched us-- me and my friend. But we weren't with the other people yet, but they were gonna take us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I bribe-- I bribed him. I wanted to bribe him. I did. I gave him my-- I had gold earrings, and I had some money. And I gave him-- I said, let me run away. Let me go-- me and my friend. He-- he took everything, and then he pulled a gun, and he said, you're not going anyplace. Then he-- I-- somehow, he left, and another one came. I had no money. I had nothing left. But I said, [POLISH], please let me go. Let us go. They said, you go-- go, then we start. So my friend said, where are you going? He's going to kill you. Why are you running? I said, let's go. Let's take a chance. So we ran. He didn't do nothing. He let us-- he let us go. So we ran in-- on-- went through the fields, and we went into a barn because we didn't want to walk during the day, and we stayed there. We say other people passing with a horse and buggy. You know, the people who they-- and they took them to Miedzyrzec.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when it got dark, I tried-- we tried to get home to my-- to Wohyn. I came home, and-- and my brother was looking for me. They-- they passed our town. They didn't see me. And they were worried about me. They didn't see me on the-- on the-- with the people. But then I came home, and I-- they were afraid they gonna look for me-- they'll come to look for me because I was there, and I disappeared. So they send me to this man again from the mill, and I was there-- I was hiding there for a few days in the attic. But they didn't come to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you go home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I went home.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so your parents, at this point, were living in the house--

speaker: interviewee
text: In the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: --of the mill owner.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, with my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which brother was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was the-- the older than me, not the oldest. The oldest had a-- a-- he had a passport-- an Irish passport, and he went to [? Warsaw. ?] And he-- like a Polack.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was your brother Eliezer that was at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were your-- Moshe and Joshua?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they-- oh, they were-- they killed them two, because he-- he wasn't-- they were in a different farm, and they didn't work because they were too young yet. But they slept in a-- also in an attic by a Gentile. And one Gentile told the Germans that two Jews are sleeping there. They took them out and killed them. They were dead already.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did your family know that they were dead?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what was the mood of your parents when they heard what had happened to your brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: You could imagine, but it was-- they were very bad, very bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were many Jews being killed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they used to come in town and take out the Jews and put them in the mart and beat them, but not-- until then, not so many. Only even the, you know, the Poles used to say-- they didn't know who was Jewish or not. The Germans didn't know, but the Poles helped them a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to your baby sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: My baby sister-- they had to take-- they gave orders right in the beginning that they should take out the-- the small children to a town Parczew-- P-A-R-C-Z-E-W. My-- my aunt-- my father's sister-- took-- went with her, and we never saw them again.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you never received any communication from them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened next to your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: One day, the Germans came into town, they-- they-- and they start shooting around the houses, near the houses. And I went into a corner. I-- I covered myself with a-- with a basin-- my face. They didn't see me, but I was in a corner sitting, but I still wanted to protect my head and face. And my brother Eliezer-- he jumped out the window, and he was shot to death.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you-- when were you able to come out of the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Soon after they left the same day. They killed a lot of people.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the-- what did the Jewish people do with the dead bodies of their family members?

speaker: interviewee
text: They buried them. We ran to look-- look for a place where to hide. But when we thought this was going on, my father had a lot of friends. They all promised him. But when it came to-- they-- all they were afraid. They didn't want to do it. So we had to come back.

speaker: interviewer
text: At one point, did the Germans make you-- make all the Jews come into a small area in your hometown? Did they form a small ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was a small ghetto. This was like in outskirts of the town. It wasn't in the center. But a few houses weren't-- weren't burned, so they let us stay there. And we weren't allowed to go all over.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there barbed wire around?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a curfew every night?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: About how many Jews lived in these-- these houses?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. Not too many. I don't know what happened to them. They went, maybe, to different towns-- there wasn't much room there-- maybe to some Gentile people. I-- I really don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- did anybody guard the Jews in these houses?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the men have to work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they took some people to work, but they sent them to camps-- to concentration camps-- to working camps, maybe. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your family next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Next, they-- they-- we went to the-- they took us to the ghetto, to Miedzyrzec.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far away was--

speaker: interviewee
text: A-- a few hours with by horse and buggy.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did they take everyone together, or did you have to hire someone?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the-- I don't remember how. We went on a horse and buggy, but I don't know what they did-- they did, you know, if they hired-- they told-- they told people to get out of town to go to Miedzyrzec. I guess, everybody hired a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a horse and buggy, and we went.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you take with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not much, not much. We didn't have much because we-- everything was burnt, and you could not buy. There were no stores.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did you stay in this ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: In this ghetto, at the end of the ghetto-- or the beginning-- there was a little house, and we stayed there. And we paid somebody to build a double wall in case something will happen, we'll hide there.

speaker: interviewer
text: We have to change tapes now.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, how did I do?
